insights: Computer assets—comprising 45.6% of the portfolio—exhibit a near-perfect inverse relationship between cost and warranty duration (r = -0.9990), meaning higher-cost computers consistently receive significantly shorter warranties, directly contradicting value-aligned procurement logic.

The top 20 highest-cost computers ($4,733–$4,971) all carry the minimum 90-day warranty, confirming a systematic policy of applying uniform minimal coverage regardless of asset value, resulting in disproportionate warranty under-provisioning for high-value assets.

This cost-warranty misalignment is universal across all seven departments and all five purchase quarters (2023-Q2 to 2024-Q2), with correlation coefficients ranging from -0.9987 to -1.0000—indicating it is a stable, organization-wide procurement practice, not a departmental or temporal anomaly.

While Computer assets have the second-highest average cost ($3,345), they receive the shortest average warranty (363 days), whereas lower-cost categories (Storage Device: $309; Computer Peripheral: $305) receive nearly double the warranty duration (780–794 days), exposing a structural mispricing of risk and lifecycle support.

The overall portfolio correlation is weakly negative (-0.1376), but this masks the dominant Computer category’s extreme inverse pattern—demonstrating that aggregated metrics obscure critical category-level procurement failures requiring targeted intervention.

Warranty quartile analysis confirms the inversion: the shortest-warranty quartile (Q1) has the highest mean cost ($3,725), while the longest-warranty quartile (Q4) has the lowest mean cost ($3,062), reinforcing that warranty allocation is decoupled from asset value and likely driven by non-economic criteria.
summary: The analysis reveals a severe, systemic misalignment in procurement strategy: high-value computer assets—nearly half the portfolio—are assigned the shortest warranties, including the absolute minimum 90-day term for the most expensive units ($4,733–$4,971). This inverse relationship (r = -0.9990) is consistent across all departments and time periods, confirming it is a deliberate, organization-wide policy—not an operational error or outlier. As a result, warranty coverage fails to scale with asset value, increasing financial and operational risk for the largest investment segment. Lower-cost categories receive proportionally stronger warranty protection, further highlighting the inconsistency in risk allocation logic. To align procurement with asset value trends, the warranty assignment framework for computers must be redesigned to reflect cost-tiered coverage, prioritizing extended warranties for high-value units. Next focus should be on auditing the procurement policy documentation and vendor contract terms governing computer warranties to identify root causes and redesign levers.
